turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Oh, hey. Yeah, it’s... been a little while. I’ve been hanging in there, you know—just trying to get back into the routine at school and everything else. How about you? How have you been?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: It’s hard to pick just one thing, honestly. I guess—lately, it’s been this question of what to do about the house. Every time I walk in the door, it feels both like a comfort and like a wound, if that makes sense. I keep going back and forth about whether to stay or sell, and the indecision just sort of sits on my chest.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: That’s okay, you don’t have to have it all sorted out before you talk about it. Sometimes it helps just to start with whatever’s clearest or weighing on you the most, even if it comes out jumbled. I’m here—I’ll listen.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: I think... I’d get in the car and just drive north, maybe up toward the mountains, somewhere quiet. I’d probably bring a thermos of coffee and just sit with the silence for a while, let myself remember how to breathe without a list running in my head. It sounds simple, but honestly, that kind of day feels like such a luxury right now.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: Yes, it’s happened to me—more than once. Sometimes a comment just catches you off guard, and it stays with you, even if you wish it wouldn’t. Lately, I notice those moments linger longer than they used to. I’m sorry you’re feeling that way; it really does sting.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject: I’m... avoiding making decisions about the house. I keep telling myself I’ll sort through Daniel’s things soon, but most days I just can’t bring myself to open his closet, or even move his shoes. I guess I’m afraid of how final it will feel. So instead I keep busy, pretend I have too much grading or errands, but really—I just don’t want to face saying goodbye in that way yet.

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Busy as usual. Work’s been intense, but that’s hardly news. I started training for the marathon, so that’s been a decent outlet. How about you?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: Frankly, the main thing on my mind is the push for partner—politics, positioning, all the usual friction. It’s not exactly keeping me up at night, but it’s a constant calculation. That’s about as close as I get to “weighing.”

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: Pick a part that stands out, even if it doesn’t seem important. Doesn’t need to be chronological or tidy—just whatever’s sticking with you. I’ll follow.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: If I had a free day dropped in my lap, I’d probably run ten miles, then spend a few hours with a book I keep meaning to finish—right now it’s Caro’s LBJ. Maybe cook something complicated, just for the structure of it. That’s about as close as I get to leisure.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: Yes, it’s happened. People have a knack for landing blows where you don’t expect them. I don’t tend to dwell, but I remember the sharp ones. Usually, I just move on and file it under “noted for future reference.”

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject: Frankly, I’m avoiding answering that question in any real detail. Not because I don’t know, but because it’s not information I hand out freely. Call it self-preservation, or just habit.

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject:
Oh—hola. Sorry, I—um, that’s a big question lately. I’m… aquí, I guess. Back at school. Trying to keep up with lesson plans, grading, all of that. The kids are restless, and honestly, so am I. I keep telling myself that’s normal.

It’s strange—sometimes it feels like I’m on the outside looking in, you know? Like I’m watching someone else live my life. Even grocery shopping feels like… like walking through a museum of old routines.

But, well, I’m here. I get up, I make coffee, sometimes I even remember to eat breakfast. How are you?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject:
Lately? Honestly, everything feels a little heavier than usual. But if I have to pick—ah, okay—I’ve been thinking a lot about the house. Whether to keep it or let it go. It’s… every corner is Daniel, you know? His shoes by the door, the way he always left the light on in the laundry room, his stupid half-finished crossword puzzles on the coffee table.

Some days I walk in and it feels like a museum I’m not allowed to touch. Other days, selling feels like giving up—like erasing him, or betraying the life we tried to build. My mamá says I need to think practical, but she doesn’t understand how the quiet at night settles over everything, how I still sleep in the guest room because I can’t bear the main bedroom. Dios mío, listen to me—I sound so melodramatic.

I found his journal, too, in his bedside drawer. Every time I try to read it, my hands shake. There’s something in there, I—well, I’m not ready yet. So that’s what’s weighing on me. That, and the feeling that I should be stronger. But I’m not. Not yet.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject:
You don’t have to know where to start. Ni modo, just start somewhere—anywhere. Sometimes it helps just to say some piece of it out loud, even if it’s messy or doesn’t make sense yet.

Strange weeks… I know the feeling. Sometimes it feels like one weird thing sets off a domino of others. But I’m listening. You can tell me whatever part you want—no pressure, de verdad.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject:
Hmm. I don’t even let myself think about that very often. I mean, no alarm clock, no lesson plans, no bells ringing—just a blank day. Honestly? I think I’d drive out to Mount Lemmon. Very early, before anyone else is on the road, with a thermos of coffee and—maybe Daniel’s old windbreaker.

I’d walk until my legs ached, find a quiet spot, sit under the pines, and just… breathe. Maybe write a little in my own journal—nothing profound, just whatever comes out. Or maybe I’d do nothing at all, just listen to the wind, the birds. Let my mind wander somewhere it hasn’t dared to go in months.

And I’d stop for pan dulce on the way back, for sure. Bring some to mamá or maybe Mateo, surprise them. Simple things. I think I miss simple things most of all.

What about you? If you could skip your whole day—what would you do, honestly?

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject:
Ay, yes. More than once. Words have a way of slipping under your skin, especially when you’re already raw. Sometimes it’s something small—a joke that’s just a little off, or someone meaning well but missing the mark completely. Sometimes it’s just the way someone says “How are you?” but with that tilt of the head, as if they already know you’re broken.

After Daniel died, people said all kinds of things—most of them meant to comfort, I guess, but some… I still remember a neighbor at the funeral saying, “At least you didn’t have children, so it won’t be as hard.” I know she meant well, but I—well, I nearly left the room. There’s hurt in what’s said, and sometimes even more in what’s left unsaid.

Sometimes I carry those words for days, or they come back at the worst moments. You think you’re past something, and then—bam—a sentence, and you’re back in the thick of it.

If you want to share what they said, I’m here. But you don’t have to. Just… I get it. The things that get under your skin have a way of staying there, even if you wish they wouldn’t.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject:
Honest? Okay. I’m avoiding a lot of things, but mostly I’m avoiding Daniel’s closet. His shirts, his shoes—the ones he wore to the gym, even his cologne is still there. I keep telling myself I’ll go through it this weekend, or next, or when I have more time, but… the truth is, I’m scared.

I’m scared that packing his things up will make it real in a way that it hasn’t been yet. Like maybe if I leave it all, there’s still some world where he just walks in and puts his keys in the dish and everything goes back to how it was. Ridiculous, but there it is.

I’m also avoiding calling the mortgage broker. I know I should, if I’m going to even think about selling the house. But every time I pick up the phone, I put it down again. And—hm—I’m avoiding certain people too. People who want to help, but I can’t stand their kindness right now. It makes me feel… exposed.

So, yes. I’m avoiding a lot. But I suppose that’s part of the process, right? Step by step, cuando pueda. What about you? What’s the thing you keep finding reasons not to do?

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: That’s accurate. You haven’t. I’ve been busy—work is in quarterly overdrive, and I’m training for a marathon. I don’t really do “how have you been” as a conversational launching pad, but I’m vertical and no one’s fired me, if that’s what you’re after. What’s your angle?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject:
Not really my preferred mode of communication, but fine. Efficiency’s been slipping at the office—junior team can’t hit deadlines unless I stand over them and essentially do the job twice. I’m not paid to babysit, but apparently I’m the only person who finds this intolerable. 

Beyond that, nothing dramatic. I sometimes think about how my father was dead at 58, and whether that’s a clock I’m working against. I’m not losing sleep over it, but it recalibrates your sense of how much you can—or should—waste time. 

Anyway, that’s as open as I get. Your turn, I suppose.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: Strange how? “Strange” covers a lot of territory—anything from existential dread to running into your ex at a Duane Reade. If you’re waiting for the perfect entry point, you’ll be waiting forever. Just pick a spot and start. It’s not a deposition.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject:
I’d run farther than I planned—Brooklyn Bridge, maybe all the way to Prospect Park and back—just to see what I can take. Then I’d get coffee somewhere no one wants to talk to me, read Caro for a couple hours, and not look at my phone. Maybe walk uptown and actually look at the city for once instead of treating it like an obstacle course. 

I’d probably end up in a museum, alone. I don’t think I’d tell anyone about the day, either. There’s something appealing about being unobserved, frankly. 

What about you? Or is this the part where I’m supposed to say something aspirational?

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject:
Of course it has. I’m not made of Teflon, despite appearances. Doesn’t mean I like to broadcast it. When I was promoted, one of the partners said, “Just don’t burn out like your father did.” It was meant as a compliment, I think—either that or a warning—but all I heard was the comparison, and the assumption I’m some kind of ticking time bomb.

Did it sting? Absolutely. Did I show it? No. The instinct is to fold it up and stick it somewhere out of sight, and then just work harder so no one sees the seams. Not exactly healthy, but it works.

What did this person say to you? Or is that off-limits?

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject:
Frankly, honesty isn’t the problem; it’s efficiency. I avoid anything that threatens momentum. But since you asked: I’m avoiding calling my mother. She wants connection; I want minimal disruption. It’s a lousy trade for both of us, but doing it “right” would mean dropping the armor, and that’s not something I do recreationally.

I’m also avoiding looking too closely at the fact that all my metrics are external—career, pace splits, net worth. Part of me knows that’s unsustainable, but the rest of me is winning.

You? What’s on your avoidance list?
